case_results.explanation: The disputed $487.32 charge qualifies as a billing error under 12 CFR § 1026.13(a)(1), which defines a billing error as a reflection on a periodic statement of an extension of credit that is not made to the consumer or to a person with actual, implied, or apparent authority to use the consumer's credit card or account. The cardholder did not make the transaction, retained physical possession of the card, and no other person had any form of authority to use the account. The remaining categories (

The case explicitly rules out every enumerated category under § 1026.13(a): the cardholder authorized the transaction (not (a)(1)), the charge was properly identified on the statement (not (a)(2)), the streaming service was delivered and accepted (not (a)(3)), there is no payment-crediting failure (not (a)(4)), no computational or accounting error exists (not (a)(5)), the cardholder did not pursue a request for clarification or documentary evidence (not (a)(6)), and the periodic statement was ma

The disputed item qualifies as a billing error under § 1026.13(a)(3), which covers extensions of credit for property or services not accepted by the consumer or not delivered as agreed. Here, the vacation rental services were not delivered as agreed because the property was uninhabitable due to undisclosed water damage, and the cardholder did not accept the services (left immediately upon discovering the condition). No designee accepted the services either. The other categories ((a)(1), (2), (4)

The transaction qualifies as a billing error under 12 CFR § 1026.13(a)(1) because it is a reflection on the periodic statement of an extension of credit that was not made to the consumer or to anyone with actual, implied, or apparent authority to use the card. The cardholder retained possession of the physical card throughout the statement period, never added authorized users, never shared the card number, and never granted use permission, establishing the transaction as unauthorized. The minor

The only potentially applicable category is § 1026.13(a)(1), which requires that the extension of credit was not made to the consumer or to a person with actual, implied, or apparent authority. The cardholder denies making the transaction, but whether the spouse—who has occasionally used the card and may have implied or apparent authority—made this specific charge has not been confirmed. Because the authorization element is unresolved, it cannot be determined whether the item qualifies as a bill

All Section 2 criteria are satisfied. 2.1: Confirmed cervical radiculopathy at C5-C6 with nerve root compression and dermatomal symptoms. 2.3: MRI within 6 months interpreted by radiologist shows pathology consistent with symptoms/surgical level. 2.2(a): 7 weeks of supervised PT (exceeds 6-week minimum) with functional outcomes. 2.2(b): Two pharmacotherapy agents (naproxen NSAID and gabapentin neuropathic) each for 5 weeks (exceeds 4-week minimum). 2.2(c): One cervical epidural steroid injection

The case fails Section 2.1 diagnosis requirement: the member has only chronic non-specific neck pain with no radiculopathy, myelopathy, or disc herniation with neurological deficit. Imaging shows only mild age-related degenerative changes with no structural pathology consistent with symptoms, failing Section 2.3. Although conservative treatment and physician documentation are complete, the absence of a qualifying diagnosis and qualifying imaging means the medical necessity criteria are not satis

The case satisfies all Section 2 criteria via the myelopathy exception pathway. Diagnosis (2.1(b)): cervical myelopathy is the primary diagnosis, confirmed by MRI showing C4-C5 cord compression with classic myelopathic signs (gait disturbance, bilateral UE weakness, hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman). Imaging (2.3): MRI within 6 months, radiologist-interpreted, consistent with symptoms and surgical level. Conservative treatment under Exception 2.2(A): PT of 4 weeks meets the reduced threshold; pha

The case satisfies all Section 2 criteria. Diagnosis 2.1(a) cervical radiculopathy is confirmed with nerve root compression and radicular pain in C5 dermatomal distribution (dermatomal symptom present; the policy requires radicular symptoms such as pain, numbness, or weakness, and pain is documented). Imaging 2.3 is met with an MRI within 6 months interpreted by a radiologist showing pathology at the surgical level. Conservative treatment is met: 7 weeks of supervised PT with functional outcomes

The case has confirmed cervical radiculopathy with supporting MRI imaging (2.1 and 2.3 satisfied) and complete physician documentation (2.4 satisfied), as well as one cervical ESI satisfying 2.2(c). However, critical evidence gaps prevent confirming 2.2(a) and 2.2(b): PT duration, supervision, and functional outcomes are not documented, and pharmacotherapy trial durations for naproxen and gabapentin are not documented (required minimum 4 weeks each). I evaluated each category under § 1026.13(a). (a)(1) unauthorized extension of credit: TRUE — the cardholder did not make the transaction and no person had actual, implied, or apparent authority. (a)(2) improper identification: FALSE — the statement complied with §§ 1026.7 and 1026.8. (a)(3) non-delivery/non-acceptance: FALSE — not at issue. (a)(4) payment-crediting failure: FALSE. (a)(5) computational/accounting error: FALSE. (a)(6) request for clarification/documentary evidence: FALSE. (a)(7)

I evaluated each of the seven enumerated billing-error categories under § 1026.13(a). (a)(1) unauthorized extension of credit: false—the cardholder confirmed they made the transaction. (a)(2) improper identification on statement: false—the charge was properly identified per Regulation Z. (a)(3) property/services not accepted or not delivered as agreed: false—the streaming service was delivered and accepted through use. (a)(4) failure to credit a payment: false—no payment-crediting issue. (a)(5)

I evaluated each enumerated billing error category under § 1026.13(a). (a)(1) unauthorized use: false—cardholder made the transaction. (a)(2) improper identification: false—properly identified per Reg Z. (a)(3) property/services not accepted or not delivered as agreed: true—the vacation rental services were not delivered as agreed (property uninhabitable due to undisclosed water damage) and the cardholder did not accept the services (left immediately upon arrival), nor did any designee. For (a)(2), identification defect under §§ 1026.7/1026.8: the merchant identifier form is not documented, making this undetermined, but the amount and date were properly reflected. Section 2.1 requires a qualifying diagnosis: cervical radiculopathy, cervical myelopathy, or cervical disc herniation with objective neurological deficit. The case explicitly negates all three — only chronic non-specific neck pain is documented. Section 2.3 requires current imaging demonstrating structural pathology consistent with symptoms; the MRI shows only mild age-related degenerative changes inconsistent with any specific symptom pattern, failing this requirement. Section 2.3 (imaging) is satisfied: MRI within 6 months interpreted by radiologist showing pathology consistent with symptoms/level. Section 2.4 (physician documentation) is satisfied: all four elements provided. Section 2.2(c) (interventional) is satisfied: one cervical epidural steroid injection.
